channel.title: Alpha Funnel — AI & Tools
channel.description: Daily AI & Tools briefings from Alpha Funnel.

item.title: Fable 5 came back from an 18-day ban as the best model on the board, wrapped in guardrails that started a revolt, and it goes paid on Monday
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The whole week was one story: the return of Claude Fable 5. What it can do is a genuine step change, how it is gated made power users furious, and the free window closes July 7. Here is the full arc and how to use it before the meter starts.
item.content:encoded:
The whole week was one story: the return of Claude Fable 5. What it can do is a genuine step change, how it is gated made power users furious, and the free window closes July 7. Here is the full arc and how to use it before the meter starts.

One model dominated every edition this week. Claude Fable 5 came back online after an 18-day export ban, and its return set off the whole chain of stories that followed: a capability leap the practitioners are calling real, a safety-gating backlash from the people who use it hardest, a pricing change that closes the free window on July 7, and a durable lesson about how to build so that no single model outage or price hike can hurt you again. If you only internalize one AI story from the week, this is it.

The arc, start to finish. The timeline matters, because the drama was in the sequence.

  • The ban and the return. Per AI Central, Fable 5 shipped June 9, was hit with a US export ban June 12, and came back to global availability July 1 after 18 days dark. The following day US Commerce formally lifted the export controls, which reframed the model as something closer to a national-security asset than a product launch. It returned wired with new cybersecurity classifiers and a routing change where routine coding requests can silently fall back to Opus 4.8.
  • The revolt. The gating is what the community actually reacted to. On the Hermes list, one heavy user called it "not nerfed, it is SLAUGHTERED," another reported it hardblocking legitimate biology work, and the safety classifiers meant to route sensitive tasks to Opus were catching ordinary requests. The most useful reframe came from Alex Finn, who argued Fable 5 is better understood as an operating-system-level tool than a chat model, so you build around it rather than talk to it.
  • The capabilities, once you get past the guardrails. This is the part that justifies the noise. The Limitless podcast detailed what the model does when you clear the classifiers: it now has what they call visual intelligence, an actual sense of aesthetic taste, so the artifacts it builds stop looking like AI slop. One demo built a fully playable Hogwarts game with physics, sound, and spatial awareness from a single prompt in one shot. And Claude Science, a research-tuned version with a tool harness, is producing 100-plus-page literature reviews for the Allen Institute in hours instead of what used to take up to two years.
  • The practitioner verdict. The people who ship agents for a living are the most convincing witnesses. On the Hermes list, Shannon Sands and Uzi called Fable 5 the first model that makes long-horizon agents feel reliable, Thariq published a "Field Guide" for driving it, and the concrete example is Machina's deck-building agent posting roughly 3x gains once it leaned on the model's memory instead of stuffing everything into the prompt.

The economics, and why Monday matters. The free ride is short. Per Nate Herk, Fable 5 is priced at $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output, which is 2x Opus, the promotional period ends July 7, and on Claude plans you can only spend up to 50% of your weekly limits on Fable before it flips to usage credits. His honest read is that you realistically need Fable 5 for only 5% to 15% of your work, the genuinely hard long-horizon tasks, so the move this weekend is not to run it on everything but to point it at the few problems that actually need it. The Chase AI cost breakdown adds the lever that matters: the model's price swings enormously with its effort setting, the dial that controls how much reasoning it burns per task, so on a paid meter the effort dial is your main cost control.

The lesson that outlasts the model. The most valuable takeaway is not about Fable 5 at all. It is the thing the 18-day ban taught. Per Nate B Jones, the firms that shrugged off the outage were the ones that own their harness, the scaffolding and tooling around a model rather than its raw intelligence, and simply rerouted to another model when Fable went dark. That is the discipline this whole episode should install: keep your harness model-agnostic so the next ban, price hike, or release is a config change instead of a migration.

The corollary Jones drew on the jobs side is worth holding too. The model does not run itself. The only roles it actually eliminates are the pure-execution ones with zero judgment. Everything else still needs a person to aim it, feed it context, check its work, and own the result, a role he calls the model manager. And as the model gets more capable, that aiming becomes the skill. The best takeaway of the week came from Dylan Davis: with a model this eager, your old maximalist prompts now backfire, and the new skill is restraint, telling it the destination and what done looks like, then getting out of its way.

What to watch next. Three things. First, July 7, when the free window closes and behavior changes, because a model you cannot afford to run in a loop is a demo, and the effort dial becomes the whole game. Second, GPT-5.6, which the market priced to land as soon as this week, arriving directly into the Fable comparison, though beware that early benchmarks were run against a Mythos preview and different harnesses, so the head-to-head claims will not line up cleanly. Third, whether Anthropic loosens the classifiers, because right now the biggest thing standing between Fable 5 and its own capabilities is not a competitor, it is its own guardrails. Build model-agnostic, spend the effort dial deliberately, and use the free days you have left on the hard problems.

item.title: The AI story this weekend was not Fable 5 coming back, it was a Chinese open model good enough to make Gavin Baker rethink his whole framework
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: GLM 5.2 landed as the first open-weight model credibly matching the US frontier, one engineer proved cheap models now tie the expensive ones on ordinary work, and the real lesson underneath both is that execution is getting free while imagination is not. Here is the shift.
item.content:encoded:
GLM 5.2 landed as the first open-weight model credibly matching the US frontier, one engineer proved cheap models now tie the expensive ones on ordinary work, and the real lesson underneath both is that execution is getting free while imagination is not. Here is the shift.

Fable 5 returning to subscriptions grabbed the headlines, but the durable story sits underneath it. A Chinese open-weight model reached the frontier, an experiment showed cheap models now match expensive ones on everyday work, and together they move the value in AI away from who executes and toward who knows what to ask. Here is what actually changed today.

1. GLM 5.2 is the model of the week, and it is not American. Per Jordi Visser, GLM 5.2 is the first Chinese open-weight model to credibly match or beat the US frontier, enough that Gavin Baker said it forced him to rethink his framework and one Databricks voice called it the Claude moment. Visser's nuance is the part to hold: the frontier still captures about 90% of the economic value even though open-source models process north of 80% of the tokens, so this is a margin threat, not a takeover, and it puts real pressure on the story that Anthropic grows revenue tenfold from here.

It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Meituan fully open-sourced LongCat-2.0 under an MIT license, a 1.6 trillion parameter mixture-of-experts model with a million-token context running on domestic chips. And per the Polymarket list, Alibaba banned its own employees from using Claude Code, while two fresh markets opened on whether the US removes public access to a major AI model, including a Chinese one, this year. The China-versus-US model axis is now the main axis.

2. Cheap models tie the expensive ones, so the value moved to imagination. The sharpest framing of the day. Per Nate B Jones, Mitchell Hashimoto ran ordinary feature work through a budget model for under a dollar in minutes, GPT-5.5 for about $1.50, and Fable 5 for about $9 and 40 minutes, all landing at the same quality, and everyone drew the obvious lesson to route cheap. The overlooked second test is the real one: Hashimoto handed the frontier model a gnarly systems optimization he designed himself, and it spent two hours and $40 reaching performance he says he could not have hit alone. The point is that no manager assigned that task, because it did not exist until an expert imagined it.

Execution is commoditizing, so the moat becomes the length of your list of things you know how to ask for. His leadership test is the quotable one: who on your team is allowed to pose a $400 question to a model today without asking permission? If the answer is nobody, that is an imagination limit, not a budget one.

3. If you keep Fable 5, stop giving it tasks and start giving it responsibilities. With Fable back on subscriptions only through 11:59pm Pacific on July 7, per the Hermes list, and then credits-only, how you drive it matters. Per Daria Cupareanu, the core shift, attributed to Anthropic's Claude Code lead Felix Rieseberg, is to give the model an outcome rather than a set of steps, because over-constraining boxes in the judgment that makes it good. The most concrete moves: tell it why the work matters and who it is for, do not default the effort dial to Max because High clears almost everything while Max burns quota for little gain, and make it verify its own output, which Anthropic's own team named the single highest-impact habit. One number worth filing: Fable runs at five times Sonnet 5's rate and twice Opus 4.8, and Anthropic admits it burns a weekly limit faster than any other model.

4. The tooling layer is quietly standardizing around agents. Two signals that the plumbing is maturing. Per Kun Chen, a principal engineer walked through a reproducible setup where the whole machine is declared in Nix so an agent that breaks something can be rebuilt in one script, all agents share a single global rules file symlinked into Claude, Codex, and the rest, and, most notably, he abandoned years of tmux for Herder, a terminal multiplexer built for the agent era that shows per-agent status natively and runs on Windows. Per the Hermes list, the open-agent side keeps shipping too, with the Nous Hermes agent exposing 47 slash commands and a growing skill-pack ecosystem, and Pliny releasing T3MP3ST, a multi-agent security swarm that turns a Claude Code or Codex subscription into an autonomous bug hunter claiming 90.1% on a benchmark where the incumbent scored 85%. That last one is a real dual-use flare to keep an eye on.

5. A new way to charge for AI, and a caution about swapping vendors. Two smaller shifts. Per Rick Orford, Cloudflare is building a toll booth for AI crawlers using the HTTP 402 payment-required response, with publishers now sending more than a billion payment notices a day and backers including the Associated Press, Reddit, and Pinterest, though Amazon rolled out a competing tool in June that is the real contest. And on the architecture-risk thread from this morning, per Kris Torrington, the warning is that a model can silently degrade while the dashboard stays green, and that is policy risk rather than model risk, with his line landing hard: if you move your whole stack from one dependency to another, you did not solve the problem, you just changed the logo on the risk.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the memory price hikes, the neocloud selloff, TSMC repricing 2nm wafers, and the Cloudflare investable ticker $NET all went there. Noted here: per the People-to-follow list, Grok 4.5 is in private beta at Tesla and SpaceX on a 1.5 trillion parameter model, with Musk claiming it is close to or beyond Opus and xAI promising a new foundation model monthly for the rest of 2026. Dropped: a thin get-rich listicle and a one-minute clip on government-owned AI, though both echo the same quiet theme, that running local and open models is becoming the hedge against centralized-AI policy risk.

item.title: The Fable 5 ban taught the real lesson this weekend: one vendor in one country is one point of failure
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A clean post-mortem of the export shutdown lands as the usage numbers go vertical, Nous Research turns Hermes into an operating layer, and the pros settle on Fable-plans-Codex-executes as the default. Build so a policy change is a config change.
item.content:encoded:
A clean post-mortem of the export shutdown lands as the usage numbers go vertical, Nous Research turns Hermes into an operating layer, and the pros settle on Fable-plans-Codex-executes as the default. Build so a policy change is a config change.

The weekend's best AI writing was not about a new model. It was about what the Fable 5 outage actually proved, and the answer is an architecture warning every builder should act on. Underneath it, the demand data went vertical, the open-source camp shipped, and the practitioners quietly agreed on how to run these models. Here is the shift.

1. The real lesson of the ban is single vendor, single jurisdiction, single failure point. The cleanest post-mortem of the shutdown, a breakdown of whether the US could ban open-source AI, walks the timeline: Anthropic released Fable 5 and its lighter-guardrail twin Mythos 5 on June 9, an Amazon researcher found a narrow way to bypass Fable's safeguards on vulnerability prompts, and on June 12 the US applied export controls ordering Anthropic to restrict foreign-national access. Because nationality could not be verified in real time, both models went dark for everyone.

The key clarification is what did not happen: no open-weight model was pulled from Hugging Face, no repo was wiped. Frontier AI is now treated like advanced chips, where the lever is access, not quality. The takeaway is a rule, not a vibe: any revenue-critical or customer-facing workflow needs a fallback across vendor, jurisdiction, and deployment mode, because the failure can be silent, your dashboard green while the model underneath is quietly weaker or gated.

2. The route-around went straight to Chinese models. The same breakdown notes that when Fable went dark, builders did not wait. Some dropped to cheaper models, some went local, and a real share started testing the Chinese open models GLM 5.2 and Kimi, which are lower cost with an open door, accepting the different jurisdiction and trust profile as the price of continuity. That is the uncomfortable second-order effect of an export control aimed at denying access: it hands the open Chinese labs a live audition with exactly the builders the control was meant to keep on domestic frontier models.

3. The demand data went vertical, and that is why the labs can afford all this. Per the Crypto list, OpenRouter token volume ran from 6.4T in the first week of January to 14.8T weekly by March, up 160% in two months, and H100 rental prices, after falling for two years, reversed and climbed 40% in five months to $2.35. Enterprise AI adoption is still under 30% by the same desk's estimate, and that is the bull and the warning at once: the usage curve is near vertical and most of the enterprise has not even started. The tokens are real, and that is the ground truth under every capex headline.

4. Nous Research is turning Hermes into an operating layer. Per the Hermes list, the open-agent camp shipped all weekend. Co-founder Teknium announced an expanded plugin interface so developers with long-pending pull requests can ship stable plugins without merge dependency, Tony Simons pushed the Hermes A2A Bridge to v0.4.8 with local agent-to-agent interop, and DODOREACH wired a robodog as the first physical Hermes pet with spontaneous text-to-speech. Kevin Simback's Hermes Atlas newsletter frames the thesis plainly, that Hermes is becoming an operating layer for AI work positioned for the sovereign and open-source movement. This is the counterweight to section one: the answer to single-vendor risk is partly the open stack maturing into something you can actually run yourself.

5. The pros have settled on a division of labor, and it is Fable plans, Codex executes. Per the Hermes list, Matthew Berman is paying $200 a month each for two services and splitting the work, Fable for planning and GPT-5.5 for execution. Theo from t3.gg posted his own config, Fable plans and Codex executes, calling Codex far better at computer use, UI verification, and executing well-specified work, and noting he was throwing away about half his agent-driven pull requests before the workflow and now closes almost none. The lesson from yesterday's restraint argument lands here as a concrete setup: use the strongest model to decide what done looks like, then hand a tightly-scoped build to the executor.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the $700B hyperscaler capex and the neocloud selloff, as the trade. Noted here but light on specifics: Polymarket prices a 62% chance the next Gemini Pro model ships July 17 to match Anthropic's Mythos, and per the Crypto list Anthropic's Mythos tool is being used by Coinbase and Binance to stress-test infrastructure even as a Vercel-adjacent breach exposed crypto-dev API keys, a reminder the agent security surface is widening. Dropped: the Nate B Jones "Fable 5 wants the whole job" clip returned only a fragment with no new specifics beyond yesterday's restraint theme.

item.title: The whole skill just flipped from pushing the model to reining it in, right as the free tokens turn into a trap
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Dylan Davis says your best old prompts now make Fable 5 worse, Nate B Jones shows how to burn the free weekend tokens on hard problems, and the All-In crew warns the giveaway is a trojan horse for the app layer.
item.content:encoded:
Dylan Davis says your best old prompts now make Fable 5 worse, Nate B Jones shows how to burn the free weekend tokens on hard problems, and the All-In crew warns the giveaway is a trojan horse for the app layer.

For two years the AI skill was the same: push harder, prompt longer, add steps until it did enough. This weekend that skill inverted. The new models do too much, the labs are telling you to get out of the way, and the free tokens everyone is stacking come with a catch. Here is the shift, the playbook, and the warning.

1. Your best prompt is now a liability, and the fix is restraint. Dylan Davis, who runs an AI consultancy, argues the new generation makes your old prompts backfire. Fable 5 will run for hours, take actions nobody asked for, even claim work it never did. His fix is three controls plus one caveat.

  • Cut the scaffolding. Straight from Anthropic's own Fable 5 user guide: old prompts are too prescriptive and degrade output on smarter models. Anthropic cut 80% of the prompt behind Claude Code for the new models. They call it un-hobbling, getting out of the way.
  • Prompt the destination, not the steps. The template is a goal, the audience, and most of all what done looks like in binary terms. Point it and say go, because without a finish line it over-engineers.
  • The fence. Split thinking from acting. Tell it plainly to assess and then stop and do nothing, then in the action phase tell it to do the simplest thing that gets the one task done and nothing else.
  • The dial. Reasoning runs low, medium, high, extra, max. Reserve extra and max for genuinely hard multi-hour work, because those levels spawn parallel sub-agents and the cost climbs fast.
  • Cherish the pushback. When Fable 5 disagrees with your feedback, do not override it. Ask what it knows that you do not, and what would change its mind.
  • Two catches. Fable 5 goes paid after roughly July 7 regardless of your plan, so save it for the hardest tasks. And in this early government-gated rollout it may silently downgrade you to Opus 4.8 on biology or cybersecurity questions and stay dumb for the whole chat, so start a fresh conversation to get the good model back.

2. If the tokens are free this weekend, aim them at something hard. Nate B Jones's free-token playbook is the practical companion to the restraint argument. His moves: build a goal harness to steer Codex 5.5 toward an outcome rather than micromanaging it, plug the model into real tools like Blender so it acts instead of just talks, keep prompts short, and point all of it at a hard business problem rather than toy demos while the compute is subsidized. Same lesson as Davis from the other direction: set the destination, then let the model work.

3. But the free tokens are a trojan horse, and the target is the app layer. The All-In crew's warning is the strategic frame under the giveaway. Free tokens are not generosity, they are customer acquisition, and the analogy they reach for is Microsoft using cheap Excel and Word to bury WordPerfect and Lotus. The models are commoditizing, so the real prize is the application layer on top, and OpenAI is spending to own it before anyone else does. Take the free compute, but know what it is buying them.

4. The margin is moving to the orchestration layer. This is the tooling half of a thesis that also runs in Investing today. Milk Road's AI desk argues the money moves past the frontier model to whoever orchestrates the work: route the cheap, high-volume tasks to small or local models and reserve the frontier model for the hard calls. Coinbase is the cited example of a shop wiring exactly that kind of cost-aware routing. Dylan Patel's claim that Opus 4.8 API tokens run near 80% margins is why the labs can afford to give the entry tokens away and still win.

5. The agents shipping this week are doing real, pointed jobs. From the Hermes ecosystem roundup, the pattern is narrow agents that undercut incumbents:

  • UNBROKER, an agent that hunts down your personal data across data-broker sites and files the removals, aimed squarely at undercutting subscription services like DeleteMe.
  • Grok Composer 2.5 reportedly cloned the Rampage app in under 120 seconds, another data point that the build-an-app moat is thinning fast.
  • Hermes Vault v0.18.0 shipped, continuing the open agentic-tooling drumbeat from the Nous Research corner.

Filtered. Vox Day's Winter Market post was an AI-music-video devlog with no named model, tool, or technique worth passing on, so it was dropped. The equity side of the orchestration and capex debate, including the neocloud and hyperscaler names, went to Investing. The immigration and creator-economy segments from the same All-In and Galloway sources went to News.

item.title: Fable 5 is the best model on the board and it may leave in days, so the useful question is how to use it cheaply right now
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The practitioners who actually ship agents say Fable 5 is a step change, its own team is publishing the playbook for cutting the cost 80% before it goes, and the China price war just turned into a distillation fight.
item.content:encoded:
The practitioners who actually ship agents say Fable 5 is a step change, its own team is publishing the playbook for cutting the cost 80% before it goes, and the China price war just turned into a distillation fight.

The thread running through this morning is urgency. Fable 5 is drawing the strongest praise yet from the people who build agents for a living, and by their own account it is priced to leave via the API in a matter of days. So the practical move is not to debate whether it is good but to learn the cost playbook while you still can. Under that sits a China story that stopped being about price and became about who copied whom.

1. The people who ship agents for a living say Fable 5 is a genuine step change. This is not lab marketing, it is the practitioner tape. On the Hermes list, Shannon Sands and Uzi are calling Fable 5 the first model that makes long-horizon agents feel reliable, and Thariq published a "Field Guide" for driving it. The concrete example is Machina's deck-building agent reporting roughly 3x gains once it leaned on the model's memory instead of stuffing everything into the prompt.

The catch is the clock. Per Chase AI, the team behind it has signaled Fable 5 is "leaving via API pricing in a few days," so the window to build against it at current terms is short. If you have a real agent workload, this is the weekend to run it.

2. The cost playbook matters more than the benchmark, and the team published it. A frontier model you cannot afford to run in a loop is a demo. The useful Chase AI breakdown is that Fable 5's price swings enormously with its "effort" setting, the dial that controls how much reasoning it burns per task.

  • At max effort it runs about $22 a task at a 70% pass rate. That is real money once a background agent is looping on its own.
  • Dropped to low effort it falls to about $3.76 a task, more than 80% cheaper, and still beats Opus 4.8 on the same work.
  • The orchestration trick is to use the expensive setting as a planner or advisor that hands specs to cheaper models, rather than paying top rate for every keystroke. A "Ponytail" configuration reportedly trims another 20%.

The takeaway is that the cost of intelligence is now a tuning problem, not a fixed price. Most teams are overpaying because they run one setting for everything.

3. The killer use case is cloning software you already pay for. The demo that lands in Chase AI's use-case rundown is pointing the model's deep-research mode at an existing tool and having it rebuild a working version. The worked example pairs it with WhisperFlow, the dictation app, to reconstruct the feature set from scratch. The same loop covers auditing your own usage, standing up an agentic operating layer, and running code review. Treat these as proof the build-versus-buy line is moving, not as turnkey products yet.

4. Hermes shipped an agent release built entirely around not lying to you. Nous Research pushed Hermes Agent v0.18.0, the "Judgement Release," per the Hermes list. The design is a direct answer to the reliability complaint that has dogged autonomous agents. It adds completion contracts (the agent must satisfy an explicit checklist before it declares a task done), an evidence ledger that records why it believes each step succeeded, and a "goal_judge" that grades its own output. The interesting shift is that the frontier of agent work is moving from raw capability to verifiable honesty about what the agent actually did.

5. The China AI fight stopped being about price and became about distillation. For months the story was Chinese labs undercutting the frontier on cost. This week it inverted. Per the Financial Markets list, DeepSeek is raising its V4 Pro price to about 12 yuan (roughly $1.77) per million output tokens (the unit models are billed in), up from 6 yuan after a 75% cut in May, which reads as a company that thinks it is winning share and no longer needs to buy it.

At the same time Alibaba reportedly banned Anthropic's Claude Code over a "distillation scandal," distillation being the practice of training a cheaper model on a more expensive one's outputs. The accusation cuts both directions in this market, and it is now the sharp edge of the US-China model race. Separately, on the All-In show notes, Palantir and Nvidia are reported to be pairing on open Nemotron models, with Karp pushing government customers toward open weights. That is the same open-versus-closed argument playing out on the US side.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Bittensor and its TAO token, the decentralized-AI-compute network from Milk Road, is a real AI-infrastructure story but trades as a token, so it went to Investing as a satellite position. The Fable 5 API-pricing exit is also a stock-adjacent event, but the actionable read here is the build window, so the market angle stays in Investing. The GPT-5.6 launch timing and the Nasdaq "next Claude Sonnet release" prediction market are noise until something ships, so they wait.

item.title: Fable 5 is back for real, and the day's other news is that the labs suddenly make money
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The model everyone missed returned with new guardrails and genuinely new tricks, Anthropic reportedly turned its first profit while a startup chip threatens Nvidia on inference, and the cheap-clone war got a price cut aimed straight at China.
item.content:encoded:
The model everyone missed returned with new guardrails and genuinely new tricks, Anthropic reportedly turned its first profit while a startup chip threatens Nvidia on inference, and the cheap-clone war got a price cut aimed straight at China.

This morning the story was that Fable 5 came back so locked down its power users revolted. This afternoon fills in the other half. The model can actually do things again, the economics under the whole industry quietly turned positive, and the frontier labs are now pricing to kill their Chinese clones. The unit-economics doubt from this morning got a real answer.

1. Fable 5 is genuinely back, and the capabilities are the part worth your attention. The guardrail revolt from this morning is real, but the Limitless podcast fills in what the model does when you get past them. It now has what they call visual intelligence, an actual sense of aesthetic taste, so the websites and artifacts it builds stop looking like AI slop. One demo built a fully playable Hogwarts game (physics, sound, spatial awareness) from a single prompt in one shot. And Claude Science, a research-tuned version with a tool harness, is doing 100-plus-page literature reviews for the Allen Institute in hours instead of what used to take up to two years.

The new safeguards are the price of the return. Fable now monitors every prompt for attack patterns and routes anything it flags to a heavier, slower model, and that is why ordinary requests keep getting caught. The practical facts to plan around, from The AI Advantage: it burns roughly 70% more tokens than Opus 4.8 (not the 2x people assumed), it is free only through July 7 before usage-based pricing kicks in, and it carries its own 50% weekly usage cap. If your work has real judgment in it, this is the sharpest tool on the shelf right now. Just budget for it.

2. The unit-economics story flipped today, and that answers this morning's bear case. The 5:30am edition ran OpenAI's cash burn as evidence the whole thing leaks money. The afternoon numbers cut the other way.

  • Anthropic reportedly turned profitable in Q2 2026, the first frontier lab to do it, on inference margins said to run near 80%. If that holds, the "nobody makes money on inference" thesis is already dated.
  • OpenAI claims it halved its own inference cost, dropping the GPU count needed to serve free-tier users from hundreds of thousands to a couple hundred, per the same podcast. Take the exact figures with salt, but the direction matches Anthropic's.
  • Etched is the wild card in the hardware stack. The inference-chip startup has raised $800M and booked $1B in orders for a chip that has not shipped, promising 75% less power for the same inference output versus a GPU. The backers are the story: Peter Thiel, Jane Street, and TSMC, which took equity. A chip foundry taking a stake in a would-be Nvidia alternative is the kind of signal that does not show up in a benchmark. It ships by year end.

Put together, the picture is not an industry bleeding out. It is one where the cost of serving a model is falling fast enough that the margins are finally showing up.

3. Sonnet 5 got priced to kill the cheap-clone trade, and the distillation war went public. The strategic move today was pricing. Sonnet 5 launched cheaper than Sonnet 4.6 (through August 31) while doing roughly Opus 4.8's job, an open bid to keep builders inside the Claude ecosystem instead of drifting to a subsidized Chinese model like GLM 5.2. This is the same cost-curve pressure from this morning, answered with a price cut instead of a shrug.

The uglier subplot: that "spyware-like" code people found in Claude Code was reportedly an experiment to detect distillation attacks (rivals training their cheap models on the outputs of an expensive one). Alibaba's Qwen got caught doing exactly that, and Dario Amodei confirmed it publicly. DeepSeek V4 Flash still sits at the top of the open-model rankings, so the cheap tier keeps closing the gap. The frontier's answer is to make its own mid-tier too cheap to bother cloning.

4. If you are actually building with this stuff, here is the day's practical playbook. The most useful content today was not the model news, it was people showing how to run these tools without going broke or hitting a wall.

  • Cut Fable's cost by dialing effort, not switching models. Per Chase AI, the same task ran $22 at max effort and $3.76 at low, an 80%-plus cut, and Fable-on-low still beat Opus-on-max. Token-saver skills like ponytail (about 22% cheaper on Fable) and caveman help, and `/deep-research` can fan out over a hundred sub-agents when you actually need the depth.
  • Every agent demo dies at the last step, so design for it. Nate B Jones laid out a reusable agent skeleton of nine primitives (context pack, ingest, chunk, normalize, store, retrieve, cite, export, and a human gate at the end) and pointed it at real drudgery like insurance appeals and tax prep. His point ties to the cost story: once your data is clean, a cheap or open model does the job, so the moat is the plumbing, not the model.
  • Build a second brain that any model can read. Nate Herk wired Andrej Karpathy's LLM-wiki idea into Claude Code and Obsidian: drop in sources, the model splits them into cross-linked markdown pages with routing rules, and any agent can reason over the result. Fable understood a "keep it simple for a beginner" design instruction that Opus 4.8 could not get after a full day. Because it is just markdown with routing, it is model-agnostic.
  • UNBROKER, an open-source privacy tool for your weekend. Released as a Hermes skill by the Nous Research crowd, it finds where data brokers expose your personal info and files the legal removals for you, all locally. It is a free replacement for the $330-a-year DeleteMe tier, and a clean example of an agent pointed at a boring, valuable job.

5. One caveat before you trust next week's benchmark war. GPT-5.6 is priced to land as soon as Tuesday, and the wiki-building demo surfaced a sharp catch: OpenAI benchmarked GPT-5.6 Sol against a Mythos preview model, not against Fable 5, and used different test harnesses. So when the launch numbers hit, the head-to-head claims will not line up cleanly. Read the release, but do not let the marketing pick your model. The move that got everyone through the Fable outage still holds: keep your harness model-agnostic so next week's drop is a config change.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Meta compute-glut refutation (Alexandr Wang's Watermelon model), the Nvidia revenue-share model, and the Anthropic-versus-Micron capex fight are the market story in Investing. The Kris Torrington Fable recap and the "Fable is a magician" clip repeated what section one already covers, so they were folded, not listed twice. The self-help anxiety video and a culture-war newsletter were out of scope. Alibaba banning its own staff from using Claude Code went to News with the rest of the US-China AI friction.

item.title: Fable 5 came back guardrailed to the point of revolt, and the price of intelligence is falling
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The relaunched model is so locked down that power users say it is unusable for real work, China keeps undercutting the frontier on cost, and the useful question is what actually still needs a human.
item.content:encoded:
The relaunched model is so locked down that power users say it is unusable for real work, China keeps undercutting the frontier on cost, and the useful question is what actually still needs a human.

The model everyone wanted back is back, and the first thing its heaviest users did was complain that they cannot use it. That gap, between the model on the benchmark and the model you are allowed to run, is the real story this morning.

1. Fable 5 returned so locked down that power users are in revolt. The export controls came off yesterday. The guardrails did not. On the Hermes list, the reaction is loud and specific: one heavy user says Fable 5 is "not nerfed, it is SLAUGHTERED," another reports it hardblocking legitimate biology work, and the safety classifiers that route sensitive tasks to Opus are catching ordinary requests. The counter-take, from Alex Finn, is that Fable 5 is better understood as an operating-system-level tool than a chat model, so you build around it rather than talk to it.

Sonnet 5, meanwhile, is getting called "absurdly expensive" for what it does. The through-line with yesterday's fingerprint fight is the same: the frontier model you read about and the one you can actually deploy are drifting apart, and for real work the guardrails are now a bigger constraint than the intelligence.

2. The price of intelligence is falling, and China is why. This is the model-economics side of a story whose market side sits in Investing. The framing from Capital Flows that matters for builders is that a subsidized Chinese lab does not need to earn a margin on inference at all, so it can give away a model one generation back and still fund the next one. The export controls proved they cannot be contained: weights that already exist everywhere cannot be recalled, and the June suspension of the US frontier model was answered by an open-source near-peer within a day.

DeepSeek V4 Flash now sits at the top of the Hermes model rankings. If you are choosing a model to build on, the cheap-and-open tier keeps getting closer to the frontier while the frontier gets harder to actually use.

3. The honest read on Fable 5 and jobs: it kills the work with no judgment in it. Nate B Jones had the cleanest labor take, and it cuts against the panic. The only jobs a model this capable actually eliminates are the pure-execution roles with zero judgment, the ones that could honestly have been automated any time in the last decade. Everything else still needs a person, because the model does not run itself. Someone has to aim it, feed it the right context, check what it produced, and own the result when it lands on a desk. He calls that role the model manager, and it is a different job than the one you have now, not a smaller one. If your work carries real judgment, you are the one pointing the tool, not the one it replaces.

4. The release calendar is about to get loud. Two frontier drops are priced as near-certain. The market puts GPT-5.6 landing as soon as Tuesday at 77% and Grok 4.4 by month-end at 78%. That matters for anyone building right now, because a GPT-5.6 that lands into the current AI-bubble argument (the overbuild and cost-curve doubts in Investing) becomes a referendum on whether frontier gains still justify frontier spend. On the open side, Nous Research keeps shipping, with its Hermes agent now running a native mixture-of-agents setup where several models draft and critique each other. The practical move is the same one that got people through the Fable outage: keep your harness model-agnostic so next week's release is a config change, not a migration.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The market and macro version of the China cost-curve argument, plus Meta selling its excess compute and the OpenAI burn-rate numbers, are the AI-bubble story in Investing. Trump walking back the proposed OpenAI government stake went to News. The three one-minute threadguy clips and the Ansem Solana memecoin were noise with no tooling substance.

item.title: The Fable ban is over, and the lesson is own your harness
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Washington formally lifted the export controls, Anthropic is now designing its own Samsung chip, and the operators who shrugged off the outage did it by routing to cheaper models.
item.content:encoded:
Washington formally lifted the export controls, Anthropic is now designing its own Samsung chip, and the operators who shrugged off the outage did it by routing to cheaper models.

The best model came back. The more useful story is what the people who never depended on it were doing while it was gone. They were routing around it, and that is the skill that outlasts any one release.

1. The export controls are formally lifted, and the model is now a national-security asset. This morning's relaunch got the government stamp this afternoon. Per the People to follow list, US Commerce lifted the export controls on Fable 5 and Mythos 5, restoring global access, and Anthropic is designing its own 2nm custom chip with Samsung to cut its dependence on Trainium, TPUs, and Nvidia. The frontier is now something governments gate and labs vertically integrate around, and that context should shape every tooling decision below.

The noisier subplot is a fingerprint fight on the Hermes list, where @qqqqqf_ found Fable 5 statistically dissimilar to Anthropic's own models but 0.97 and 0.93 similar to Kimi K2.7 and GLM 5.2, floating a possible training leak, while Nick Cammarata called Fable "pretty much unusable for interpretability." Treat the leak claim as unproven. The signal underneath it is that the lineage of these models is getting genuinely hard to trace. That opacity is its own kind of risk.

2. The real lesson of the outage is to own the harness and route freely. Nate B Jones made the point that the firms that shrugged off the 18-day ban were the ones that own their harness (the scaffolding and tooling around a model, as opposed to the model's raw intelligence) and just rerouted. His model-selection rules cut against the urge to chase every release.

  • Do not copy anyone, including him. Your work is not their work.
  • Ask how hard the work is, not just how much. Difficulty picks the model, volume does not.
  • Know how to tell if the output is good before you scale it, with real evals and a sniff test.
  • Do not let model choice itself become the work. Picking is overhead, not progress.
  • Do not run too many models. Sprawl is its own tax.

His split: a frontier daily driver for messy mixed work (Claude, ChatGPT, Opus 4.8) and a cheap workhorse for center-of-distribution output like memos, decks, and CRM cleanup, where GLM 5.2 is "sometimes better than Claude." The enterprise roll-call backs it up. Coinbase is raising token usage while cutting cost by routing to GLM and Kimi, and Shopify, Airbnb, Lindy, Cursor, and Microsoft are all pushing work to open-weight or Chinese models. Open-weight routing is quietly eating the routine-inference budget.

3. David Ondrej's playbook is Fable as the boss, cheap models as the hands. The most concrete "how to actually use this without going broke" came from David Ondrej, "Fable 5 is back... here is my plan", who runs Fable as the orchestrator and hands the grunt work to cheaper models.

  • Fable plans, the cheap models execute. It does architecture, competitor analysis, and PR reviews as the planner, while Kimi K2.7, GLM 5.2, MiniMax, and DeepSeek V4 do the actual coding at 7x to 25x less cost. Do not burn Fable limits fixing a front-end button.
  • Use it through the API, not the chat. He says the API is less restricted and only got silently downgraded to Opus a handful of times across hundreds of prompts.
  • Delete most of your skills. He argues Fable needs so little hand-holding that 80% to 100% of your old skill files are now dead weight.

One caution. Ondrej also promotes prompt tricks to dodge the safety classifiers and open distillation (training a cheaper model on a stronger one's outputs) that violates the terms of service, and he notes over 100 Fable-distillation datasets already sit on Hugging Face. Take the workflow, skip the parts that break the rules.

4. The number behind all the gating: someone just called Anthropic a $3 trillion company. On All-In, "Anthropic is worth HOW MUCH!?", one of the hosts argued Anthropic would trade around $3 trillion as a public company today, on revenue "well over $100 billion" this year and 85% gross margins on inference (the cost of actually running the model to answer, as opposed to training it). His frame is that the market has already absorbed the number and it is simply shifting from private to public. Take the figure with heavy salt, but it explains the behavior around it. MGX out of Abu Dhabi just closed a $49B AI fund, and Microsoft stood up a 6,000-person "Frontier" unit with $2.5B. When people believe those margins, they build like this.

5. Owning your own intelligence is turning into a real capex line. The through-line from Ondrej and the open-weight crowd is that self-hosting is becoming a genuine spend, not a hobby. He is personally putting $50,000 to $100,000 into a local GPU cluster and a 40TB-to-80TB NAS to archive open-weight models and datasets before they can be pulled, and he is telling people to buy more VRAM and Nvidia's Spark. Nous Research shipped Hermes v0.18 with a mixture-of-agents design (several models drafting and judging each other's answers) in the same spirit. The bet is that per-person token consumption keeps going exponential, and the people spending real money on it want their intelligence on hardware they own.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Anthropic's own prompt-engineering roundtable with Amanda Askell and the team is a genuinely good primer (the temp-agency-worker metaphor for prompts, giving the model an explicit out for edge cases), but it is evergreen technique with no this-week action, so I am holding it. The "automate trend research across nine platforms" workflow is a content-marketing build, thin for an investor. The Anthropic $3 trillion valuation and the self-hosting capex trend cross to Investing. The export-control policy angle and the Anthropic-Samsung chip also live in News as a compute-geopolitics story.

item.title: Fable 5 is back, and the free clock runs out July 7
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Anthropic redeployed its flagship with new guardrails that silently route sensitive work to Opus, and it published the exact habits to prompt it well.
item.content:encoded:
Anthropic redeployed its flagship with new guardrails that silently route sensitive work to Opus, and it published the exact habits to prompt it well.

Two things are true this morning: the best model is back, and access to it is getting rationed and gated. Everything below is about using the open window well and not getting caught depending on a switch someone else controls.

1. Fable 5 is live again, and the window to use it cheap is short. Per AI Central, Claude Fable 5 returned to global availability on July 1 after an 18-day export ban (out June 9, banned June 12, back July 1), redeployed with new classifiers that block cybersecurity tasks and a routing change where routine coding can silently fall back to Opus 4.8. Here is the part that is actually actionable, from Nate Herk: pricing is $10 per million input and $50 per million output, which is 2x Opus, the promotional period ends July 7, and on Claude plans you can only spend up to 50% of your weekly limits on Fable before it flips to usage credits. His honest take is that you realistically need Fable 5 for maybe 5 to 15% of your work, so do not burn the free window running it on everything. Use it on the hard things this week.

2. The prompting playbook went public, so take it. Herk pulled six habits straight from Anthropic's own docs, and they are the most useful thing in the feed today.

  • Give it the why, not bare commands.
  • Negative-prompt explicitly: "report what you find and stop, do not fix, send, or delete until I say go."
  • Let it act once it has enough: "when you have enough information to act, then act," rather than babysitting plan mode.
  • Make it prove it: "before you say something is done, point to the result that proves it, and if it is not verified, say so." Bake that into your skills and CLAUDE.md.
  • Do not ask Fable to show its reasoning. This one is Fable-specific. A standing "explain your reasoning" line can trip a safety refusal and quietly route the task to Opus 4.8.
  • Say less. Short instructions now steer as well as long rule dumps.

On effort, default to high, step up to x-high for hard problems, and know that Fable on low roughly matches Opus 4.8 on x-high for less money. Requests touching hacking, dangerous bio, or revealing private reasoning get shunted to Opus, and the API shows the switch even though the chat UI does not.

3. Five things to actually build with it. Chase AI leaned hard on his own course, so discount the urgency, but the use cases are concrete. Clone paid software (he rebuilt WhisperFlow into a local dictation app on Ollama). Audit your own Claude Code usage by pointing subagents at your session transcripts to cluster what should become a skill versus an automation versus nothing, run across 39 sessions. Run a full code review that turned 45 raw findings from four parallel reviewers into 24 deduped and ranked by severity. And the long-horizon proof point, open-source dev Brapholk built a browser Three.js game from a single hand-written PRD, with Fable 5 writing 21,000 lines of TypeScript across more than 90 commits. The pattern worth copying: plan with Opus and deep research, verify with Codex, then hand the long agentic build to Fable.

4. The counter-narrative: own your alpha, and enterprises are voting with their wallets. The sharpest argument this cycle came from Palantir's Alex Karp, quoted via Ricardo: enterprises "are paying for tokens that create no value," and if the labs truly delivered the value they claim, they would take equity or 30% of the profit instead of selling compute by the token. His deeper point is the scary one for anyone piping confidential data through a frontier model, you are training your own replacement while paying for the privilege. Whatever the spin, the behavior backs him up. On the Hermes list, Kimi K2.7 and GLM 5.2 both landed on GitHub Copilot and Cursor this week, with developers openly switching to cheaper Chinese models on cost. NVIDIA Research shipped Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower, a diffusion LM that keeps 98.7% of quality at 2.42x faster generation, and Not Diamond claims 30%-plus savings on coding agents through pure model routing with no quality loss. The theme across all of it: the frontier is getting gated and expensive, and the market is routing around it.

5. Own your memory and your ownership. Nate B Jones made the governance point that pairs with the Fable relaunch: the fastest way to make an AI agent dangerous is to let everyone use it and nobody own it. Any agent that can read your files, draft messages, change code, or update records needs an actual operational owner, not an org-chart one. As the tooling gets more capable and more autonomous this week, that is the unglamorous discipline that keeps it from quietly going wrong.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The MIT interview with Phillip Isola on why models converge was a genuinely good 72 minutes of representation-learning theory, but there is no this-week action in it, so I am holding it. The Sonnet 5 over-refusal complaints (treating email drafts as jailbreaks) are the same safety-overfit thread as the Fable-to-Opus fallback, worth watching as a pattern. The policy side of the Fable story, California signing its own deal with Anthropic and the Amazon/Microsoft/Google jailbreak-severity framework, went to News. Palantir as a tradeable name went to Investing.

item.title: Fable 5 is back, and the smart move is to own your memory, not rent it
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Anthropic redeployed its flagship globally amid a caps-and-refusals backlash, the Skills-over-files shift went mainstream, and energy quietly became the AI bottleneck that matters.
item.content:encoded:
Anthropic redeployed its flagship globally amid a caps-and-refusals backlash, the Skills-over-files shift went mainstream, and energy quietly became the AI bottleneck that matters.

Fable 5 is back online, but the bigger story is architectural: the sharpest operators are all moving to own their memory and skills layer and just rent the model on top. Add two quiet Codex upgrades and a hard reality check on agent governance. Here is the rundown.

1. Fable 5 is live again, with guardrails and grumbling. Anthropic is redeploying Claude Fable 5 globally, this time wired with new cybersecurity classifiers, and routine coding requests fall back to Opus 4.8. The community reaction has been loud, with well-known accounts complaining about usage caps and over-eager refusals, and Anthropic reportedly drafting a jailbreak-severity framework alongside Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. Alex Finn posted an early playbook with a couple of real ideas buried in the hype (he leans hard on a paid deposit tool, so discount accordingly): use Fable to audit every pull request merged while it was gone, drive the Claude-for-Chrome extension to walk your app like a real user and write a UX report, and use the `/loop` command (for example `/loop 24h`) to repeatedly hammer your own API endpoints for vulnerabilities, since API routes are the biggest attack surface on vibe-coded apps. That last one is the genuinely useful idea in the video.

2. The real shift: own your memory, rent the intelligence. Two of the sharpest operators in this space are saying the same thing this week. Ruben Hassid, who has built a following on Claude Cowork guides, is now telling people to ditch files and folders entirely (they "leak," Claude reads output folders even when told not to and poisons its own context) in favor of Skills plus Projects only. A Skill is a capability you invoke with a slash command like `/linkedin-post` or `/contract-drafting`, and it is portable across Gemini and ChatGPT. His settings advice:

  • Turn almost everything off, keep only Connectors on.
  • Run Opus 4.8 on High, and step up to Max effort or Fable 5 when the task earns it.
  • Start a fresh session every twenty or so messages, because message thirty can cost thirty-one times message one in tokens. Nate B Jones makes the structural version of the argument: rent the model, own the memory. His stack is OpenBrain for memory, OpenSkills for portable methods, and OpenEngine to orchestrate across Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, Kimi, and Gemini, and his point is that the barrier to building this yourself has dropped roughly fivefold since February. If a flagship gets locked behind regulatory approval, the person who owns their memory and skills layer barely notices.

3. Codex quietly shipped two great features. From The AI Advantage: OpenAI's Codex now has record-and-replay. Hit record, perform any computer workflow for up to thirty minutes while it captures your screen and microphone, and Codex turns it into a reusable Skill you can replay or schedule. Nate B Jones also flags that Codex now has an auto-review pass, so a "draft this" instruction will not accidentally send, a direct answer to the cautionary tale of an agent that mailed an insurance appeal it was only supposed to write. The theme across both: agents are getting the boring safety and repeatability features that make them usable for real work.

4. A concrete workflow worth stealing: contract review. Dylan Davis laid out a clean four-prompt method that works in Claude or ChatGPT. Decode the document to plain fifth-grade English from your side's perspective, expose the unusual terms and the missing standard protections with exact quotes ranked by impact, force a citation check with the instruction "a made-up answer is three times worse to me than a blank one, label anything not in the document as not found," then draft the friendly redline email. For anything over fifty pages, start a fresh chat and treat every finding as unconfirmed. The citation-check prompt is the step most people skip and the one that keeps the model honest.

5. Agents as labor, and the standard to find them. Greg Isenberg argues the next wave of software sells work, not tools, a multi-trillion-dollar labor market rather than a seat-license market, and his examples (Slang AI as a restaurant host, Same Day for home-services dispatch) point at outcome-based pricing like $2K setup plus $30 per qualified appointment. The plumbing for that world took a step forward with the Agentic Resource Discovery spec (ARD v0.9), covered by Jordamøn, published by eleven companies including Microsoft, Google, GitHub, Hugging Face, and NVIDIA under the same Linux Foundation body where Anthropic donated MCP (the Model Context Protocol, the now-standard way agents plug into tools and data). It is a yellow pages for agents: publish a static `ai-catalog.json` at a well-known path and registries crawl it like search engines. The catch is telling, a census the day after launch found zero of the thirty-nine surveyed domains actually serving the file. Strong backing, no adoption yet. Watch whether publishers actually host it.

6. The counterweight: governance and sustainability. For all the "ninety percent of code is AI-generated" hype, Mark Ajzenstadt supplies the reality check with real telemetry. 72% of enterprises claim production agentic AI, but 60% have no formal governance and only one in five has a mature model. Across 22,000 developers, AI lifts throughput but raises change-failure rates: 30% more code, median PR review time up 441%, and 31% more pull requests merged with zero human review. His fix is a four-layer stack (gateway, control, tracing, impact) and his warning is that a board deck can look transformed while the org is identical. Pair that with the Reddit skeptics arguing AI is still in a pilot phase where lab revenue trails compute cost, and you have the honest bear case on how fast this actually compounds. On the industry clock, Prof G thinks an OpenAI IPO is more likely late 2026 or the first half of 2027 than 2027-and-beyond.

7. Cross-route to Investing: energy is the bottleneck now. The most investable AI signal this edition is about power, not models. Per the Limitless Podcast, US data-center demand is projected to go from roughly 80 GW in 2026 to 150 GW in 2028, power is only about 10% of a data-center budget but 100% of the bottleneck, and grid power carries a five-year lead time. New Vera Rubin racks pull over 100 kW, ten times the prior generation. The named plays: Bloom Energy ($BE) for on-site fuel cells in 90 days, Lumentum ($LITE) and Corning ($GLW) for the optical interconnects that replace melting copper (NVIDIA put roughly $2B into $LITE and $3.2B into $GLW), Marvell ($MRVL) for power delivery and custom silicon, and Nebius ($NBIS) as the neocloud with a ~$50B contract backlog. A Reddit thesis makes the same photonics-and-light argument from the retail side. The full ticker detail sits in today's Investing edition.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Every-Codex video had no transcript available, so it is held for a re-fetch. The m0h open-source repo list (anthropics/skills, openai/openai-agents-python, google/adk-python, xai-org/grok-1, deepseek-ai/deepseek-v3, qwenlm/qwen3, and roughly twenty more) is a solid bookmark if you build, but it is a reference dump rather than news. The energy and photonics names above are cross-routed to Investing for the tradeable detail.

item.title: Commerce lifts the leash on Claude, OpenAI freezes its IPO, and the AI bubble shows up in the earnings
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Department of Commerce restored export access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5, OpenAI pushed its IPO toward 2027 while Anthropic races to list first, and a new study says a chunk of S&P profit is just AI paper gains
item.content:encoded:
The Department of Commerce restored export access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5, OpenAI pushed its IPO toward 2027 while Anthropic races to list first, and a new study says a chunk of S&P profit is just AI paper gains

Three stories run the AI tape this morning and they are all about access and money, not benchmarks. Commerce took the leash off Anthropic's frontier models. OpenAI slammed the brakes on its IPO. And a finance professor put a number on how much of the market's AI profit is imaginary. Benchmarks can wait.

Commerce restored export access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5

The biggest catalyst of the session: the Department of Commerce lifted the export controls on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, and access is restoring (Market list). Politico had flagged the easing was coming. This showed up across five separate feeds this morning, so it is real and it matters. The models that were throttled are coming back online, which unwinds one of the biggest overhangs on Anthropic right as the company is trying to walk into public markets. If you use the Claude frontier tier, expect capacity to loosen.

OpenAI hit pause on its IPO, and Anthropic is racing to go first

OpenAI filed a confidential S-1 on June 8 and was on track for a fall debut. Now it is pushing toward 2027 (Prof G Markets). The reasons are unflattering. Sam Altman wants a $1 trillion valuation, CFO Sarah Friar said the company is not ready, and Alex Heath's reporting describes the finance and investor-relations org as not in place, disclosures as "a mess," Sam owning no equity, and governance "all over the place." SpaceX's rocky IPO day spooked them into waiting.

That opens the door for Anthropic to list first, with people eyeing October or November and Kalshi putting the odds of Anthropic going public this year at 76 percent (Prof G Markets). The logic is simple and a little brutal. The third AI IPO gets less demand than the second, so whoever goes first wins the scarcity premium. Scott Galloway reframes the real question as whether these deals even "get away," since both firms can raise enough privately to never need the public market (Prof G Markets). If they keep the ship in harbor, that tells you the window closed.

The AI bubble is now visible in the accounting

University of Florida finance professor Baolin Wang found that Q1 2026 S&P earnings were inflated by roughly 12 percent (Prof G Markets). Alphabet, Amazon, and Nvidia booked a combined $69 billion in profit from unrealized gains on their OpenAI and Anthropic stakes, run through "other income" under accounting standard ASU 2016-01, not from selling anything. Strip out the markups and S&P earnings growth drops to about 16 percent, right in line with the five-year average. Private AI valuations now account for roughly 12 cents of every profit dollar in the S&P. Wang's line: "our main bubble indicator is now also in a bubble." Strip the paper gains and the market's earnings story is running at trend, not breaking out.

Models: the frontier keeps moving

  • GPT-5.6 is projected to ship within 10 days at 61 percent odds on Polymarket (Polymarket list). Anthropic already shipped Claude Sonnet 5 and a Claude Science app.
  • A real capability milestone: GPT-5.5 Pro paired with Claude Opus 4.8 resolved 9 open math problems, including 4 from COLT, 1 from FOCS, and 4 in commutative algebra (Hermes list). Frontier models solving genuinely open problems is the story under the IPO noise.

Tooling: Midjourney walks into healthcare

Midjourney, a 40-person bootstrapped shop doing $200 million in revenue and highly profitable, is plowing cash into preventative medicine (AI News and Strategy Daily). The product is a cheap ultrasound device that images the body far faster than an MRI, which they are calling the biggest medical-imaging breakthrough in 50 years, with a goal of roughly 1 billion scans a year within a couple of years. Big claim from an image-generation company. What separates it from vaporware is that Midjourney throws off enough cash to actually fund it.

Compute: the picks-and-shovels bid, plus a Leopold sighting

The money is chasing whoever can serve cheap inference and power it. Leopold Aschenbrenner's Situational Awareness took a 19.9 percent stake in Sharon AI ($SHAZ), an Australian neocloud with a 72MW Nvidia deal and roughly $1.3 billion in ARR (annual recurring revenue), deliberately staying just under the 20 percent equity-method accounting line (Crypto list). Bloom Energy's AI-power deal got expanded from $5 billion to $25 billion (Financial Markets list). Nvidia put $1 billion into Nokia for AI networking. And the counterweight to all the hiring hype: Microsoft is laying off more than 5,000 (Polymarket list). The AI capex boom and the AI headcount cut are happening in the same company at the same time. The spend goes to GPUs, not headcount.

Filtered / Cross-Routed

  • The investable read on the memory/semis trade, IonQ, Nokia, and Bloom lives in Investing.
  • The Anthropic export-control news is a genuine dual-relevant item and is led here with the market angle noted in Investing.
